Name:
Mr. Hood
(as Zev Love X with KMD)
Notes:
(1988) (Zev Love X, DJ Subroc, & Jade 1 (Rodan) form KMD, with Jade 1 leaving soon after to focus on school, being replaced with Onyx the Birthstone Kid.)
(May 14, 1991) (KMD's debut album Mr. Hood releases commercially.)
Type: KMD (Kausing Much Damage, or a positive Kause in a Much Damaged society) was a Hip Hop group in the early 90s perhaps best known for launching the career of MF Doom (known during his KMD tenure as Zev Love X). After guesting on 3rd Bass The Gas Face, the trio (Zev, brother Subroc, and Onyx) released the acclaimed and overlooked Mr. Hood full-length. Their political outlook was similar to the group Brand Nubian, who guested on Hood; however, the style was more comical and cutting edge and ahead of its time with its production and skits from KMD and Stimulated Dummies (John Gamble and Mr. Dante Ross). This full-length LP features the classic singles, Peachfuzz, Who Me? and Nitty Gritty (feat. Brand Nubian).

Name:
BL_CK B_ST_RDS
(as Zev Love X with KMD)
Notes:
(March ?, 1993) (Black Bastards starts production)
(April 23, 1993) (DJ Subroc dies while attempting to cross the 878 Nassau Expressway)
(May ?, 1993 - August ?, 1993) (Zev Love X would finish up the album)
(May ?, 1994) (Elecktra cancels the release of the album and drops KMD from their label due to controversey surrounding the cover art.)
(May 15, 2000) (Black Bastards would officially release)
Type: Following their 1991 debut album, Mr. Hood, the former trio shed one member leaving only two remaining – Subroc and his brother, Zev Love X (better known today as MF DOOM). Originally scheduled for release in 1994, their sophomore album Black Bastards showed clear progression from their debut. Sadly, Subroc would face a sudden and untimely death in 1993, just as the duo were finishing the album. Grief-stricken, his brother Zev Love X – now the sole remaining member of the group – was determined to carry the legacy of KMD onward, but Elektra Records unceremoniously shelved the project in the eleventh hour, due to controversy surrounding the album’s provocative cover art. Following the fallout with Elektra, Zev tried for years to release the album on other labels, but he was continually met with dead ends, until eventually, it was released in 2000 through Readyrock.

Operation: Doomsday
Notes:
(May-Sept 1994) (The loss of his brother DJ Subroc and complications with Elecktra Records refusing to release BL_CK B_ST_RDS causes Zev Love X to quietly step away from the hip-hop scene.)
(October 19, 1999) (Having comeback from his multi-year hiatus, Daniel Dumile has created a new character, MF DOOM, & releases his debut album under the name.)
Type: The 1999 release of Operation: Doomsday marked MF DOOM’s official debut, reintroducing a mysterious figure who would soon become one of underground rap’s greatest voices. Within its 19 tracks, Operation: Doomsday reveals the confluence of DOOM’s tragic past, personal interests and daring creativity. His clever rhymes and remarkable schemes stood out against the landscape, and every sound he touched —from cartoon theme songs, to ‘80s soul, to rap classics and more — got reinterpreted into something brand new and surreal.

Type: After releasing his debut album under the MF DOOM moniker, Operation: Doomsday, DOOM took his first multi-year hiatus from album work, focusing mainly on producing, although he did release an EP with MF Grimm, along with a number of loose singles and features. This time period sees an extension of his Operation sound, before he became truly villainous. This break signified a 5 year break between studio albums that he would release under the MF DOOM moniker, with his first 4 albums after this hiatus all being released under "alter egos".

Name:
Vaudeville Villain
(as Viktor Vaughn)
Notes:
(November 2002) (The unfinished demo of Madvillainy is stolen & leaked, frustrating both DOOM & Madlib, and causing them to work on their own individual projects for the time being.)
(September 16, 2003) (Vaudeville Villain releases comercially.)
Type: Vaudeville Villain tells a story of Viktor Vaughn, who was described by NME as a "time travelling street hustler". The character was named after Marvel supervillain Victor Von Doom. While time traveling, Viktor Vaughn's time machine got damaged, which forced him to stay in the early 1990s New York City. The album shows his day-to-day life, with each track showing a different situation he ends up in as he tries to earn money to fix his time machine.

Name:
Madvillainy
(Collaboration with Madlib as Madvillain)
Notes:
(2002) (Madvillainy starts production.)
(November 2002) (The unfinished demo of Madvillainy is stolen & leaked, frustrating both DOOM & Madlib, and causing them to work on their own individual projects for the time being.)
(March 23, 2004) (Madvillainy releases commercially.)
Type: Two years in the making, and combining two of the best underground Hip-Hop artists of the early 2000s, this collaboration between MF DOOM and Madlib has been appropriately dubbed Madvillainy. With both men known for their stunning soundscapes, outlandish rhyming skills, and schizophrenic personalities, the duo decided to have Madlib concentrate on the beats and let DOOM handle the rhymes. In mixing their comic-book-like personas, Madlib and DOOM play to their nearly superhuman strengths, perhaps best exemplified by the singles “America’s Most Blunted” and “All Caps”. Madlib’s inventive production leans towards the jazzy side of his repertoire, and DOOM unleashes outrageous lyrics on par with some of his best efforts. Never flashy or glamorous, Madvillainy is served straight up, the way Hip-Hop was meant to be.

(as Viktor Vaughn)
(VV:2)
Notes:
(Start Date) (Start Action)
(Aug 3, 2004) (Venomous Villain releases commerically.)
Type: VV:2, also known as Venomous Villain, is the fourth studio album by British-American rapper and producer MF Doom, and the second and final album to be released under his alias Viktor Vaughn. It was released through Insomniac, Inc. on August 3, 2004. The sophomore effort adds to the dark world created by Viktor Vaughn on Vaudeville Villain while expanding the character. To be completely transparent, even DOOM himself said that he didn't think the album was great. That being said, there's still noteworthy material that spawned from this effort, such as tracks like "Fall Back / Titty Fat", "Pop Quiz (Extra Credit Remix)", & "Doper Skiller" which features DOOM's only collaboration with Kool Keith, which is, frankly, a crying shame.

Name: MM..FOOD
Notes:
(???) (MM..FOOD begins production.)
(November 16, 2004) (MM..FOOD releases commercially.)
Type: MM..FOOD finds DOOM digging deep into vocal soundbytes related, as well as fitting to his namesake and artistic vision, meticulously building a cohesive album narrative. Diligence in mind, his writing is as sharp, witty and often shocking as ever. Production-wise, DOOM utilizes a fine blend of familiar and obscure sample sources, complimentarily working together to both satisfy and intrigue. And, while MM..FOOD is mostly the work of a mad scientist in his secret lair, a sprinkling of super-villain team ups with Count Bass D, Madlib, PNS of The Molemen, Stahhr, and Mr. Fantastik, all add to the album's overall flavor.

Name:
⭐️ Beef Rapp 
(Beef Rap)
Notes: Track 1 on MM.. FOOD. Samples "Would You Like A Snack?" by Frank Zappa, & the episode "Canon of Doom" from the 1982 Spider-Man cartoon series. The opening dialogue is from the 1983 film "Wild Style".

Name: ⭐️Hoe Cakes
Notes: Track 2 on MM.. FOOD. Released as a single on November 9th, 2004. Samples "Jane" by EPMD, "New York, New York" by Starship Orchestra, & the episode "Canon of Doom" from the 1982 Spider-Man cartoon series. Also features sound effects from DOOM for the PlayStation. The instrumental for this track is named "Jasmine Blossoms", & is from MF DOOM's album "Special Herbs 4, 5, 6", released under his "Metal Fingers" moniker.

Name:
Potholderz 
(feat. Count Bass D)
(prod. Count Bass D)
Notes: Track 3 on MM.. FOOD. Released as the B-Side to the "Hoe Cakes" single on November 9th, 2004. Features Count Bass D, who is also credited as a producer on the track. Samples "400 Girls Ago" by Billy Butler, "Still D.R.E." by Dr. Dre feat. Snoop Dogg, "Ain't No Half-Steppin'" by Big Daddy Kane, "Jane" by EPMD, & the episode "A Short Time Later" from The New Fantastic Four cartoon series. Weirdly, this track is listed as track 4 on the 2007 vinyl edition of this album. This change is not present anywhere else.

Name:
⭐️ One Beer
(prod. Madlib)
Notes: Track 4 on MM.. FOOD. Released as a limited press 7" single on March 30th, 2004. Features production from Madlib. Famously samples "Huit Octobre 1971" by Cortex, along with "You Go to My Head" by Brian Ferry. Also features sound effects from DOOM for the PlayStation. Interpolates "I Get a Kick Out of You" by Ethel Merman, "Nobody Move, Nobody Get Hurt" by Yellowman, & "Teletubbies Say 'Eh-oh!'" by The Teletubbies. Features dialogue from the episodes "Time Travelled" & "Get a Good Grip on My Feet, Ben" from The New Fantastic Four cartoon series, the episodes "UN Meeting" & "Dr. Doom, Master of the World" from the 1981 Spider-Man cartoon series, & "Late Night Show Debut Episode" from the Late Show with David Letterman TV show. One Beer was supposed to be included on the debut album between Madlib & MF DOOM, Madvillainy, but was scrapped for unknown reasons & later included on MM.. FOOD, albeit credited only to MF DOOM, & not Madvillain. Weirdly, this track is listed as track 3 on the 2007 vinyl edition of this album. This change is not present anywhere else.

Name: ⭐️ Deep Fried Frenz
Notes: Track 5 on MM.. FOOD. Samples "Out of the Blue" by Caldera. Features dialogue from the 1970 film "Watermelon Man", the episode "The Fantastic Four Meet Doctor Doom" from The New Fantastic Four cartoon series, & the episode "Canon of Doom" from the 1981 Spider-Man cartoon series. The instrumental for this track is named "Myrrh", & is from MF DOOM's album "Special Herbs Vol. 1 & 2", released under his "Metal Fingers" moniker.

Name: Poo-Putt Platter
Notes: Track 6 on MM.. FOOD. 1st of 4 skits present on the album. Samples The Fat Albert Halloween Special. Features dialogue from the 1973 film "Hell Up in Harlem", the episode "The Malt Shop" from the Scooby Doo, Where Are You! cartoon series, the episode "Euell Gibbons Spoof" from The Electric Company TV show, & the episodes "The Invisibles", "Energy Creature", & "Space Trappers" from The Herculoids cartoon series. The instrumental for this track is named "Datura Stramonium", & is from MF DOOM's album "Special Herbs, Vol. 9 + 0", released under his "Metal Fingers" moniker. Famously, Joey Bada$$ used the same instrumental for the track "World Domination", off of his 2012 mixtape "1999".

Name: Fillet-O-Rapper
Notes: Track 7 on MM.. FOOD. 2nd of 4 skits present on the album. Samples "Mean Lene" by Hubert Laws. Features dialogue from the 1973 film "Hell Up in Harlem", & the episode "Euell Gibbons Spoof" from The Electric Company TV show.

Name: Gumbo
Notes: Track 8 on MM.. FOOD. 3rd of 4 skits present on the album. Samples "Would You Like a Snack?" by Frank Zappa. Features dialogue from the 1973 film "Hell Up in Harlem", & the episode "Euell Gibbons Spoof" from The Electric Company TV show.

Name: Fig Leaf Bi-Carbonate
Notes: Track 9 on MM.. FOOD. 4th of 4 skits present on the album. Samples Out of the Blue" by Caldera, "Hot Wind" by Les Baxter, the episode "The Devious Dr. Dumpty" from the 1981 Spider-Man cartoon series, & the episode "0514" from the Sesame Street TV show. Features dialogue from the episode "Euell Gibbons Spoof" from The Electric Company TV show, the episode "Search Order From Satan - Find The man with the seven scars!" from the Fist of the North Star anime series, & the episode "The Fantastic Four Meet Doctor Doom" from The New Fantastic Four cartoon series. The instrumental for this track is named "Dragon's Blood", & is from MF DOOM's album "Special Herbs 4, 5, 6" released under his "Metal Fingers" moniker.

Name: ✨ Kon Karne
Notes: Track 10 on MM.. FOOD. Samples "Is It a Crime" by Sade & "Latoya" by Just-Ice. Features dialogue from the episode "Canon of Doom" from the 1981 Spider-Man cartoon series.

Name:
Guinnessez
(feat. Anglelike, 4-Ize)
Notes: Track 11 on MM.. FOOD. Features Anglelike & 4-Ize. Samples "Louisiana Blues" by Climax Blues Band. Features dialogue from the reading of the comic "Return of Doctor Doom", from The Marvel Comics Radio Series, the episode "0349" from the Sesame Street TV show, the episode "Stick Finances" from The Electric Company TV show, the episode "UN Meeting" from the 1981 Spider-Man cartoon series, the episode "Jeepers, It's the Creeper" from the Scooby Doo, Where Are You! cartoon series, & the 1961 film "A Raisin in the Sun". The instrumental for this track is named "Lovage", & is from MF DOOM's album "Special Herbs, Vol. 2" released under his "Metal Fingers" moniker.

Name:
⭐️ Kon Queso
(prod. PNS)
Notes: Track 12 on MM.. FOOD. Produced by PNS. This song was originally recorded & released on a 12" under the name "Yee Haw", with DOOM rerecording & renaming it to fit with the food themes of the album. Samples "Newscast One" by Christian Chevallier & John Tender. Features dialogue from the episode "Canon of Doom" from the 1981 Spider-Man cartoon series, & the episode "The Three Predictions of Dr. Doom" from the 1967 Fantastic Four cartoon series.

Name:
⭐️ Rapp Snitch Knishes
(feat. Mr. Fantastik)
Notes: Track 13 on MM.. FOOD. Features Mr. Fantastik. Interpolates "Rampage" by EPMD. The instrumental for this track is named "Coffin Nails", & is from MF DOOM's album "Special Herbs, Vols. 5 & 6", released under his "Metal Fingers" moniker.

Name: Vomitspit
Notes: Track 14 on MM.. FOOD. The idea for this track was first formed during the "Operation: Doomsday" sessions, at the time simply being titled "Vomit". Samples "Happy You Should Be" by Mashmakhan, & "You Go to My Head" by Bryan Ferry. Features dialogue from the episode "The Fantastic Four Meet Doctor Doom" from The New Fantastic Four cartoon series. There is an older version of this song, recorded before MM.. FOOD released, as indicated by the title of the 2006 release "Vomit Original (Demo Mix)" between MF DOOM & Parallel Thought, although it is unknown exactly when this was recorded, with the only information we have coming from Parallel Thought's Bandcamp, stating "Old ass single we did with DOOM. Paid with bar mitzvah cash. Our version was recorded before he reused the vocals for 'Vomitspit' which appeared on MM... Food".

Name: ⭐️ Kookies (Physical Version)
Notes: Track 15 on the original pressing of MM.. FOOD. Samples "Go Stetsa I" by Stetsasonic, & the episode "0514" from the Sesame Street TV series. Features dialogue from the episode "Dr. Doom, Master of the World" from the 1981 Spider-Man cartoon series, & the episode "The Fantastic Four Meet Doctor Doom" from The New Fantastic Four cartoon series. The instrumental for this track is named "Horehound", & is from MF DOOM's album "Special Blends", released under his "Metal Fingers" moniker. This version of the song is not present on any streaming services due to the Sesame Street sample being uncleared for streaming. This version was only present on the original pressing of the album, until it was put onto the physical version of the 20th Anniversary Edition release of the album.

Name: Kookies (Streaming Version)
Notes: Track 15 on streaming service versions of MM.. FOOD. Samples "Go Stetsa I" by Stetsasonic, "Angel of Love" by Mike Longo, & "The Adoration" by The Electric Prunes. Features dialogue from the episode "Canon of Doom" from the 1981 Spider-Man cartoon series. This is the version of the song present on all streaming services, along with every physical pressing, excluding the first pressing, & the 20th Anniversary Edition pressing. Has a more "stripped back" instrumental, seemingly being made from the ground up to comply with sample clearances. Also features a different vocal take.

The end of the interview has him talking to the listener, telling them that there is going to be no shortage of upcoming music from him.

Name:

(Collaboration with Danger Mouse as DANGERDOOM)
Notes:
(2003) (The Mouse and the Mask begins production and recording.)
(Oct 17, 2005) (The Mouse and the Mask releases commercially in the United States.)
Type: Back in 2005, super-producer Danger Mouse and the masked Hip-Hop super-villain DOOM came together for The Mouse and the Mask, a collaborative album released under the name DANGERDOOM. Among MF DOOM’s most successful projects, both critically and commercially, the album was inspired by Cartoon Network’s late night Adult Swim programming. The Mouse and the Mask also includes guest appearances from several Adult Swim characters, including Aqua Teen Hunger Force, Space Ghost, Harvey Birdman, and more, as well as guest features from Talib Kweli, Ghostface Killah, and Cee Lo Green.

Name: Works From 2006 - 2008
Notes:
(Start Date) (Start Action)
(End Date) (End Action)
Type: After his collaboration effort with Danger Mouse, "The Mouse and the Mask", we had to wait 4 years for DOOM's next true studio album to release. That's not to say that he wasn't working on music during this multi-year period, as there was a number of projects he was confirmed to be working on during this period. One rather infamous one was a project between him and J Dilla, but after Dilla's unfortunate passing in February of 2006, it was put on hold indefinitely. He was also doing some work for the infamous sequel to Madvillainy, but that work never turned into anything more than a couple singles. He did release a number of efforts throughout this period however, such as a live album, and arguably went on his best run of features, with standout tracks such as John Robinson's "Sorcerers", Talib Kweli's "Fly That Knot", & the "Chinatown Wars" remix, with Ghostface Killah.

Name:
⭐️Sniper Elite
(prod. J Dilla)
Notes: In late 2005, MF DOOM and Ghostface Killah were recording over beats for J Dilla's upcoming album "Donuts". After J Dilla's untimely passing 3 days after Donuts released, Ghostface Killah included some of the work in his 2006 album "Fishscales", while DOOM postponed the Dilla-Doom project indefinitely and took a short break from making new music. This track was originally intended to be included on Donuts, being combined with the song "Murder Goons" to make one mixed together song. After not being included in the tracklist, Stones Throw would release "Sniperlite" as a single in 2008, featuring those two songs unmixed, with a mixed version serving as a bonus track. Murder Goons was a song for Ghostface Killah, with no DOOM involvement, so it will not be featured on this tracker.

Name:
Sniper Elite & Murder Goons (Bonus)
(w/ Ghostface Killah)
(prod. J Dilla)
Notes: This is the mixed together version of the two songs "Sniper Elite" & "Murder Goons", both originally intended to be included on J Dilla's 2006 album "Donuts", mixed together as one long song. They ended up getting scrapped, and after J Dilla's untimely passing 3 days after Donuts released, Stones Throw would keep them in their back pocket until 2008, where they would release "Sniperlite" as a single, featuring the two songs unmixed, with this mixed together version serving as a bonus track.

(Collaboration with Madlib as Madvillain)
(aka stones throw's least funny joke ever)
Notes:
(Start Date) (Start Action)
(End Date) (End Action)
Type: Madvillainy 2: The Madlib Remix was originally released in CD form in Madvillainy 2: The Box (2008), and released *only* with the box set, but Stones Throw eventually decided to make it official. Some might say it’s sacrilege to revisit an album like Madvillainy, but Madlib’s never been one to adhere to convention. Like many of his best works, he made this one for himself – headphone listening pleasure for a ten-hour plane ride to Tokyo. But this reworking was so good that after sitting with it for a while, Stones Throw decided they had to make it available to fans. Kind of a giant middle finger naming it "Madvillainy 2" though.

Name:
BORN LIKE THIS
(as DOOM)
Notes:
(Start Date) (Start Action)
(March 24, 2009) (BORN LIKE THIS releases commercially.)
Type: Born Like This is the third solo album released by Daniel Dumile as his MF DOOM persona, his 6th solo album overall, and the final solo album he released before his death on October 31st, 2020. Interestingly, this is the only album where “MF DOOM” is shortened to “DOOM.” In addition to tracks produced by DOOM, the album includes production by from frequent collaborator Madlib, as Madvillain, as well as the late J Dilla. The album title is borrowed from Charles Bukowski’s poem, “Dinosauria, We”, which employs the phrase as a cadence. A reading of the poem by Bukowski himself is used to introduce the track “Cellz.” It is an aggressive album that follows a loose storyline, and its lyricism involves verbal braggadocio and social commentary on police brutality and the ghetto condition.

Type: NehruvianDOOM is Bishop Nehru’s debut album. Produced by MF DOOM and featuring the super-villain on several tracks. This is a compact project with only nine tracks, clocking in at 30 minutes long. Bishop Nehru’s breakthrough mixtapes, released from the early age of 15, saw him rhyming over DOOM produced instrumentals already. Once DOOM joined the line of MC’s who co-signed the young rapper – including heavyweight lyricists Kendrick Lamar and Nas, the latter of which introduced him on stage during his set at SXSW as “the future of music” – it seemed a no-brainer for the two to work together. The result was a stellar  collaborative project, NehruvianDOOM.

Name:
The Missing Notebook Rhymes
(Collaboration Effort with Adult Swim)
Notes:
(Aug 7, 2017) (The Missing Notebook Rhymes would be announced and the first track from it would get posted on adultswim.com for their Adult Swim Singles program.)
(Sep 26, 2017) (Adult Swim would abruptly announce the cancellation of the project, taking the already-uploaded tracks off of their website, and formally ending their partnership with MF DOOM. While it has never been confirmed, evidence suggests that it was either sample clearance issues, or Malakai Dumile's rapidly declining health.)
Type: August 7, 2017, MF DOOM’s The Missing Notebook Rhymes project was announced via Adult Swim. As stated in the original post on GasDrawls.com: "Adult Swim received a folder from DOOM called The Missing Notebook Rhymes […] Each track is considered a “notebook” from a series consisting of music from [DOOM’s] own upcoming albums as well as singles he is featured on […] One notebook will be released per week over the course of the next 15 weeks". With this post and announcement came “Notebook 00 – Negus” by Sean Price, featuring MF DOOM. In addition to Sean Price, The Missing Notebook Rhymes features material from DOOM’s origins in K.M.D., as well as material from Jay Electronica, The Alchemist, Astronote, Kool Keith, and MF DOOM’s scientist-alias, Viktor Vaughn. The weekly releases were cancelled after the seventh week when, out of nowhere, Adult Swim announced that they were ending their partnership with DOOM.

Type: Rising from the wreckage of a war-torn planet, Czarface (controlled by Wu-Tang Clan’s Inspectah Deck and 7L & Esoteric) joins forces with MF DOOM in the epic Czarface Meets Metal Face! Blending DOOM’s trademark abstractions with Czarface’s in-your-face lyrical attack, this album is ripe with cartoon violence, societal observations and pop culture musings. Over banging beats provided by The Czar-Keys, the armored team give you witty, unpredictable treats that any Hip-Hop fan can sink their fangs into. This album packs a punch with 16 tracks, including features from Open Mike Eagle and  Jedi Mind Tricks’ Vinnie Paz.

Name:
Super What?
(Collaboration with Czarface)
Notes:
(Apr 18, 2020) (Super What? is supposed to release on Record Store Day 2020, but is pushed back due to COVID-19, & is finished in the summer of 2020.)
(Oct 31, 2020) (Daniel Dumile passes away from angioedema following a reaction to a blood pressure medication.)
(May 7, 2021) (CZARFACE releases Super What? with the blessing of DOOM's estate, marking his first posthumous release.)
Type: Super What? is the second collaboration album between rap trio CZARFACE (formed of 7L & Esoteric and Wu-Tang Clan member Inspectah Deck) and the late underground legend MF DOOM. Released on May 7, 2021, it comes three years after their first joint project, CZARFACE Meets Metal Face, and represents MF DOOM’s first posthumous album since he passed away in October 2020. According to Esoteric, the album was originally meant to drop in April 2020, but it was pushed back due to the COVID-19 pandemic, and then finished in the summer of the same year. In a similar fashion as some of CZARFACE’s previous works (for example, the 2015 effort Every Hero Needs a Villain), Super What? heavily incorporates cartoon samples and features many comic-themed lyrics, both elements that were often used by MF DOOM throughout his discography.

Type: After DOOM's unfortunate passing at the end of October in 2020, the majority of releases that his name is involved with have been left up to his estate. Shortly after his passing, CZARFACE released "Super What?", marking his first posthumous release. Since then, it has been fairly quiet, with the only notable releases since then being 20th Anniversary Edition of "MM… FOOD", and the streaming release of "Madvillainy Demos", although the latter was through Stones Throw. Rest in peace to the SUPAVILLAIN, we miss you and will never take the influence you have had on the musical medium for granted.
